```text
III-13-82 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident InvestigationSting T-2000, Pipistrel Virus and Sinus Aircraft and Ultralight Aircraft13.16.15 The Sting TL-2000 aircraft uses the rocket-powered Galaxy Recovery Systems (GRS) installation as dothe Pipistrel Virus and Sinus aircraft. This system is installed in the rear cabin area of the aircraft and projects theparachute through the rear cabin window area. Once the parachute has been deployed, the rocket continues beyond thecanopy until the propellant is spent and then falls away to the ground.13.16.16 Other ultralight aircraft use one of several styles of parachute depending on the type of aircraft. Some ofthese systems deploy in an upward direction, while others deploy downward or rearward. Systems from BRS, GRS andothers were identified as installed in these aircraft. The BRS website reveals a list of over 100 different mountinginstallations, in both ultralight and other types of aircraft such as hang gliders and gyrocopters.Danger markings and accident site safety13.16.17 There are a variety of warning markings on aircraft to indicate the presence of the parachute systems. Onthe Cirrus aircraft there is a small black text warning that is placed adjacent to the unmarked exit point for the parachute(see Figure III-13-13). The largest size text on the warning is about 6 mm high. The Cirrus warning is not conspicuousand could easily be overlooked following an accident.13.16.18 The Cirrus warning decal states the following (see Figure III-13-14):WARNING!ROCKET FOR PARACHUTE DEPLOYMENT INSIDESTAY CLEAR WHEN AIRPLANE IS OCCUPIEDThere are no warning markings printed on the rocket motor canister. There are also no markings on the aircraft’sfuselage to delineate the exit path of the forward harness straps on the aircraft, or that clearly mark the outline of theconcealed hatch above the parachute.______________________
```

```text
III-14-1Chapter 14MAINTENANCE INVESTIGATION14.1 PURPOSE14.1.1 A maintenance investigation reviews the maintenance history of the airplane, the actions of themaintenance organization and its staff and the programme under which it was maintained to determine:a) whether the aircraft has been maintained in accordance with the applicable airworthiness andmaintenance regulations of the cognizant licensing authority, including the suitability of suchregulations;b) the relevance of maintenance information to the accident that may suggest particular lines ofinvestigation; andc) any maintenance actions or materiel factors which were a contributing cause or a root cause of theaccident/incident.14.2 SCOPE14.2.1 A maintenance investigation encompasses three categories; adequacy of maintenance performed,maintenance management capability and human factors.14.2.2 The investigation is a thorough examination of extensive details, particularly in the case of accidentsinvolving a transport category airplane. Principle areas to be examined include:a) The operating history of the airframe, engines, propellers and components. This includes any eventsresulting in defects or irregular operations that have occurred.b) The aircraft records, to establish:1) That all applicable airworthiness directives have been incorporated in the airplane.2) That all major repairs and alterations incorporated on the airplane were performed in accordancewith applicable regulations and accepted industry practice and were approved by the State ofRegistry.3) That the approved maintenance programme has been followed correctly.4) That the administrative and technical procedures of the maintenance programme used complywith the regulations of the State of Registry.5) That any discrepancies or omissions associated with the airplane have been properly corrected.
```

```text
III-14-2 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigation14.3 INVESTIGATING MAINTENANCE ERROR14.3.1 Like operational errors, maintenance errors rarely occur in isolation. Investigators who suspect or discoverone or more maintenance errors must delve deeply into all factors that may have contributed to the final error(s). Thefollowing paragraphs represent many of the factors to be evaluated but are not an exhaustive list.Information14.3.2 Information refers to the written or computerized source data that a maintenance technician needs to carryout a task or job. It includes workcards, maintenance manual procedures, service bulletins or engineering orders,maintenance tips, illustrated parts catalogues and other manufacturer supplied or internal resources. To determine thatinformation was a contributing factor to the maintenance error, either the information itself must be problematical(e.g. hard to understand, not complete, conflicting), or the information should have been used but was not (e.g. it wasnot available, it was ignored). If it is expected that the maintenance technician has this information memorized, then referto the Technical Knowledge/Skills section.14.3.3 For any particular source of maintenance information that is considered worthy of further evaluation lookfor the following conditions:a) Not understandable;1) Unfamiliar words or acronyms;2) Unusual or non-standard format;3) Poor or insufficient illustrations;4) Not enough detail or missing steps; and5) Poorly written procedures;b) Unavailable/inaccessible;1) Procedure does not exist;2) Not located in correct or usual place; and3) Not located near worksite;c) Incorrect;1) Missing pages or revisions;2) Does not match airplane configuration;3) Transferred from source document incorrectly;4) Steps out of sequence;
```

```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-35) Not the most current revision; and6) Procedure does not work;d) Too much/conflicting information;1) Similar procedures in different resources do not agree (e.g. MM versus task card);2) Too many references to other documents;3) Configurations shown in different resources do not agree; and4) Similar action for two different applications (e.g. hydraulic supply and return lines attach tohousing next to each other with same size and colour of fitting allowing easy misinstallation);e) Update process is too long/complicated;1) Requested revisions have not been incorporated yet;2) Configurations changed by Service Bulletins or Engineering Orders have not been updated inapplicable maintenance procedures; and3) Document change requests are not submitted or are lost or incorrectly filled out;f) Incorrectly modified manufacturer’s MM/SB;1) Intent of manufacturer’s procedure is not met;2) Non-standard practices or steps are added; and3) Format does not match rest of procedure or other procedures;g) Information not used;1) Procedure available but ignored; and2) Technician too familiar with procedure; andh) Other;1) Operator cannot use digital information (this may be more likely at outstations).Equipment/tools/parts14.3.4 Equipment and tools refer to things such as non-destructive test equipment, work stands, calibrated torquewrenches, screwdrivers, test boxes, and special tools called out in maintenance procedures. Parts refer to airplane partsthat are to be replaced.
```

```text
III-14-4 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigation14.3.5 Unsafe equipment and tools may cause a maintenance technician to become distracted from the task dueto concern for personal safety. If equipment or tools are not available or are inaccessible, the maintenance technicianmay use other equipment or tools that are not fully suited for the job. Other factors that can contribute to error includemiscalibrated instruments, use of unreliable equipment, or equipment or tools with no instructions for use.14.3.6 Incorrectly labeled parts can contribute to improper installation or repair. Parts that are unavailable cancontribute to error by the maintenance technician who uses a substitute part, or may even omit parts such as shims.14.3.7 If equipment, tools or parts are suspected, investigate for the following conditions:a) Unsafe;1) Platform moves and is unstable;2) Brakes or safety devices inoperative;3) Non-skid material worn or missing;4) A lock-out mechanism is missing or faulty;5) Placards (warnings or cautions) are missing or faded;6) Sharp edges are exposed or personal protective devices are missing; and7) Power sources are not labelled or protected;b) Inaccessible;1) Not in usual location (possibly being used on other task or airplane); and2) Too far away from the worksite;c) Unreliable;1) Intermittent or fluctuating readings on dials or indicators;2) Damaged or worn out;3) Expired use limits; and4) Part with history of defects;d) Poor layout of controls or displays;1) Easy to read wrong display or use wrong control;2) Awkward locations, hard to reach;3) Too small to read or control; and4) Directional control of knobs or dials is not clear;
```

```text
III-14-6 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigation2) Wrong part number on tool or part; and3) Illegible labels;l) Not used;1) Equipment/tool/part is available but not used; and2) Not all parts installed during multiple installation; andm) Other;1) System protection devices on tools/equipment not available; and2) Parts are fragile, damaged during installation.Airplane design or configuration14.3.8 An airplane should be designed and configured so that parts and systems are accessible for maintenance.The maintenance technician should be able to reach a part, should be able to remove it, and should be able to easilyreplace the part in the correct orientation. When reviewing accessibility as a contributor to maintenance error, it must beseen as a real contributor to the error and not just as an inconvenience to the maintenance technician.14.3.9 Good designs also incorporate feedback that helps the maintenance technician know that something hasbeen performed correctly. For example, an electrical connector that has a ratchet effect provides feedback to themaintenance technician when the installation is correct. If this ratchet effect is included in some connectors and notothers, this could contribute to error. If a maintenance technician goes from a ratchet connector to a non-ratchetconnector, the technician may over-tighten the second connector looking for the ratchet.14.3.10 Configuration variability between models and airplanes can contribute to error when there are smalldifferences between the configurations that require maintenance tasks to be carried out differently or require slightlydifferent parts.14.3.11 Consider if aircraft design or configuration contributed to suspected maintenance errors when investigatorsfind any of the following conditions:a) Complex;1) Fault isolation on the system or component is difficult;2) Installation of components is confusing, long, or error prone;3) Multiple similar connections exist on the system or component (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic,etc.);4) Installation tests for the component are extensive and confusing; and5) Different sized fasteners can be installed in multiple locations;
```

```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-7b) Inaccessible;1) Components or area to be maintained is surrounded by structure;2) No access doors exist in the maintenance area;3) Area lacks footing space or hand-holds; and4) Small or oddly-shaped area;c) Not user friendly;1) Lack of feedback provided by component or system;2) Can be easily installed with wrong orientation; and3) Direction of flow indicators do not exist;d) Configuration variability between models/airplanes;1) Similar parts on different models are installed differently; and2) Airplane modifications have changed installation or other maintenance procedures betweenairplanes; ande) Other;1) Components are too heavy for easy removal/installation.The maintenance job/task14.3.12 A maintenance technician’s work can logically be separated into a series of tasks. Under certaincircumstances, when the tasks are poorly planned and/or combined, the work can quickly become unmanageable. If theinvestigator feels the task was a contributing factor, the investigator should analyze the combination or sequence oftasks. The investigator, when examining the task sequencing, should also determine whether written information wasbeing used and what technical skills and knowledge were expected of the maintenance technician.14.3.13 Determine whether the particular job or task involved any of the following conditions:a) Repetitive/monotonous;1) Similar steps are performed over and over (opening and closing circuit breakers during a longtest); and2) The same task performed many times in multiple locations (removing seats, inspecting lap jointsfor cracks);b) Complex/confusing;1) Multiple other tasks are required during this task;
```

### 376

**📊 元数据**

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-9Maintenance technician/engineer technical knowledge/skills14.3.14 Technical skills (sometimes also referred to as abilities) refer to tasks or subtasks that maintenancetechnicians are expected to perform without having to refer to other information. Technical skills include such things asbeing able to lock wire, use a torque wrench, and remove common parts from an airplane. For (lack of) technical skills tobe a contributing factor to error, the technician must be lacking a skill that was generally expected of the technician.14.3.15 Technical knowledge refers to the understanding of a body of information that is applied directly toperforming a task. Technical knowledge, in order to be a contributing factor to error, is knowledge that is supposed to beknown (memorized) by the maintenance technician. Three broad categories of knowledge are required of a technician:airline process knowledge, airplane systems knowledge, and maintenance task knowledge. These are discussed inmore detail below.14.3.16 Airline process knowledge refers to knowledge of the processes and practices of the airline or repairstation in which the maintenance technician works. Examples include shift handover procedures, parts taggingrequirements, and sign-off requirements. While this knowledge is generally acquired through general maintenanceoperating procedures and on-the-job discussion with peers, it may also be acquired from other sources such asemployee bulletins and special training.14.3.17 Aircraft system knowledge refers to knowledge of the physical aircraft systems and equipment. Examplesinclude location and function of hydraulic pumps and rework options for corroded or damaged parts. While thisknowledge is generally acquired from the airplane design characteristics, training, maintenance manuals, and on-the-jobdiscussion with peers, it may also be acquired from other sources such as trade journals and maintenance tips.14.3.18 Maintenance task knowledge refers to the specific knowledge required to perform a unique task. Examplesinclude the procedure for bleeding a hydraulic system and limits for wear of a tire. While this knowledge is generallyacquired through maintenance instructions or on-the-job discussions with peers, it may also be acquired from airplaneplacards, design characteristics, or even other maintenance technicians when working as a team.14.3.19 When investigating the technical competency of the maintenance technician/engineer, look for evidence of:a) Inadequate skills;1) History of similar, frequently made errors;2) Poorly installed or serviced items when procedures were available and straightforward;3) History of poor performance after extensive training in tasks, airline processes, and airplanesystems; and4) Trouble with memory items or poor decision making;b) Inadequate task knowledge;1) Slow task completion;2) Technician change of maintenance responsibilities;3) Task performed by maintenance technician for the first time; and4) Task performed in wrong sequence;
```

- -> 下一块: [[#377\|377]]

### 377

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落377`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 3255 (约1953 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-10 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigationc) Inadequate airline process knowledge;1) Failure to acquire parts on time;2) Technician new to airline or to type of work (from line to hangar, etc.); and3) Airline processes not documented or stressed in training;d) Inadequate airplane system knowledge;1) Technician changes airplane types or major systems; and2) Fault isolation takes too much time or is incomplete; ande) Other;1) Technician performance/skills not accurately tracked/measured; and2) The expectations and performance of the technician’s peers – would another technician havedone the same thing?Factors affecting individual performance14.3.20 Factors affecting individual performance vary from person to person and include factors brought to the jobby individuals (e.g., body size/strength, health, and personal events) and those caused by outside factors (e.g. peerpressure, time constraints, and fatigue caused by the job itself). These factors may help explain errors made bymaintenance technicians who are usually top performers.14.3.21 Physical health includes the acuity of human senses as well as physical conditions and physical illnesses.Human senses, especially vision, hearing, and touch, play an important role in maintenance. Technicians are frequentlyrequired to perform tasks that are at or near the limits of their sensory capabilities. For example, some tasks requiregood vision and/or touch, such as visual inspection for cracks or finger inspection for burrs. Good hearing is alsorequired in order to hear instructions or feedback before and during a maintenance task.14.3.22 Physical conditions, such as headaches and chronic pain, also have been shown to relate to errors.Alcohol/drug use, as well as side effects of various prescription and over-the-counter medicines, can negatively affectthe senses. Physical illness, such as having a cold or the flu, can also negatively affect the senses and the ability toconcentrate. Illnesses can also lead to less energy, which can affect fatigue.14.3.23 Fatigue has been defined by the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) as a depletion of body energyreserves, leading to below-par performance. Fatigue may be emotional or physical in origin. Acute fatigue may becaused by emotional stress, depletion of physical energy, lack of sleep, lack of food, poor physical health, or overexcitement. Fatigue may also be caused by the work situation itself. The time of the day, the length of time one has beenworking, and complex mental tasks or very physical tasks can cause fatigue.14.3.24 Time constraints or time “pressure” are common to the maintenance technician. The need to finish amaintenance task so an airplane can be released from the gate or to finish a heavy maintenance task so an airplane canbe put back into service often causes technicians to feel pressure to get their tasks done. Studies have linked both toolittle time and too much time with increased error. There is a well known speed/accuracy trade-off, in that the faster onetries to finish a task the more likely an error is to happen. This trade-off also holds for speed and safety. However, whenthings are done too slowly, boredom can set in and also increase the chance of errors.
```

Peerpressure may also influence a technician’s safety-related behaviour.14.3.26 Body size and strength are two obvious factors that affect a maintenance technician’s ability to perform atask. If someone is too small to reach a plug or if someone is unable to let down an LRU from an upper rack, this cancontribute to error.a) Physical health:1) Sensory acuity (e.g. vision loss, hearing loss, touch);2) Failure to wear corrective lenses;3) Failure to use hearing aids or ear plugs;4) Restricted field of vision due to protective eye equipment;5) Pre-existing disease;6) Personal injury;7) Chronic pain limiting range of movement;8) Nutritional factors (missed meals, poor diet);9) Adverse affects of medication;10) Drug or alcohol use;11) Complaints of frequent muscle/soft tissue injury; and12) Chronic joint pain in hands/arms/knees.b) Fatigue:1) Lack of sleep;2) Emotional stress (e.g. tension, anxiety, depression);3) Judgment errors;4) Inadequate vigilance, attention span, alertness;5) Inability to concentrate;6) Slow reaction time;7) Significant increase in work hours or change in conditions;8) Excessive length of work day;

For example, temperature extremes (eithertoo hot or too cold), high noise levels, inadequate lighting (reflection/glare, etc.), unusual vibrations, and dirty worksurfaces could all potentially lead to maintenance errors. Concerns about health and safety issues could also contributeto maintenance technician errors. Look for:a) High noise levels;1) High noise impacts the communication necessary to perform a task;2) Extended exposure to noise reduces ability to concentrate and makes one tired; and3) Noise covers up system feedback during a test;b) Hot environment;1) Work area is too hot so the task is carried out too quickly;2) Extremely high temperatures cause fatigue;3) Long exposure to direct sunlight; and4) Exterior components or structure too hot for maintenance technicians to physically handle or workon;

```text
III-14-16 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigationn) Other;1) Area(s) not organized efficiently (difficult to find parts, work cards, etc.); and2) Area too crowded with maintenance technicians and/or other personnel.Organizational environmental issues14.3.28 The organizational culture can have a great impact on maintenance error. Factors such as internalcommunication with support organizations, trust level between management and maintenance technicians, managementgoals and technician awareness and support of those goals, union activities and attitudes, morale, etc., all affectproductivity and quality or work. The amount of ownership the technician has of the work environment and the ability tochange or improve processes and systems is of key importance to the technicians’ morale and self esteem which, in turn,affects the qualify of task performance. Examples to look for:a) Quality of support from technical organizations;1) Inconsistent quality of support information;2) Late or missing support information;3) Poor or unrealistic maintenance plans;4) Poor or missing task pre-planning;5) Lack of feedback on change requests;6) Reluctance to make technical decisions; and7) Frequent changes in company procedures and maintenance programmes;b) Company policies/work processes;1) Unfair or inconsistent application of company policies;2) Standard policies do not exist or are not emphasized;3) Standard error prevention strategies don’t exist or are not applied;4) Inflexibility in considering special circumstances;5) Lack of ability to change or update policies; and6) Policies inhibit reporting of errors and therefore inhibit process improvements;c) Unions;1) Contract negotiations create distractions;2) Historical management/labour relations are not good;
```

```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-173) Positive or negative communication from union leadership; and4) Strike, work slowdown, or other labour action creates a disruption;d) Unstable work force;1) Layoffs are occurring;2) Early retirement programmes drain experience;3) Reorganizations, consolidations and transfers cause more people to be in new jobs;4) Demotions and pay cuts; and5) Frequent management changes; ande) Other;1) Company is acquired by another company;2) Work previously accomplished in-house is contracted out; and3) Overall inadequate staffing levels.Leadership and supervision14.3.29 Even though supervisors normally do not perform the tasks, they still contribute to maintenance error bypoor planning, prioritizing, and organizing of job tasks. Delegation of tasks is a very important supervisory skill and if notdone properly, can result in poor work quality. Also, there is a direct link between the management/supervisory attitudesand expectations of the maintenance technician and the quality of the work that is performed.14.3.30 Also, supervisors and higher-level management must also provide leadership. That is, they should have avision of where the maintenance function should be headed and how it will get there. In addition, leadership is exhibitedby management “walking the talk,” that is, showing the same type of behaviour expected of others.14.3.31 Investigators should make themselves aware of leadership and supervision inadequacies:a) Poor planning/organization of tasks;1) Excessive downtime between tasks;2) Not enough time between tasks;3) Paperwork is disorganized;4) Tasks are not in a logical sequence; and5) Airplane configurations are changed too frequently during the maintenance visit;
```

```text
III-14-18 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigationb) Inadequate prioritization of work;1) Technicians not told which tasks to carry out first;2) Important or safety related tasks are scheduled last; and3) Fault isolation is not performed with the most likely causes checked first;c) Inadequate delegation/assignment of tasks;1) Assigning the wrong person to carry out a task;2) Inconsistency or lack of processes for delegating tasks;3) Giving the same task to the same person consistently; and4) Wide variance in workload among maintenance technicians or departments;d) Unrealistic attitude/expectations;1) Frequent dissatisfaction, anger, and arguments between a supervisor and a technician about howto do a task or how quickly a task should be finished;2) Pressure on maintenance technicians to finish tasks sooner than possible or reasonable;3) Berating individuals, especially in front of others;4) Zero tolerance for errors; and5) No overall performance expectations of maintenance staff based on management vision;e) Excessive supervision;1) “Look over the shoulder” management style (micromanagement);2) Frequent questioning of decisions made; and3) Failure to involve employees in decision making; andf) Other;1) Not enough supervision;2) Meetings do not have purpose or agendas;3) Supervisor does not have confidence in group’s abilities; and4) Management doesn’t “walk the talk” and thereby sets poor work standards for maintenance staff.
```

```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-19Communication issues14.3.32 Communication issues refer to breakdowns in communication that prevent the maintenance technicianfrom getting the correct information regarding a maintenance task in a timely manner. The types of communicationinclude both written and verbal.14.3.33 Issues which influence effective maintenance actions include communications:a) Between departments;1) Written communication incomplete or vague;2) Information not routed to the correct groups;3) Department responsibilities not clearly defined or communicated;4) Personality conflicts create barriers to communication between departments; and5) Information not provided at all or not in time to use;b) Between people (peers);1) Failure to communicate important information;2) Misinterpretation of words, intent or tone of voice;3) Language barriers;4) Use of slang or unfamiliar terms;5) Use of unfamiliar acronyms;6) Failure to question actions when necessary;7) Failure to offer ideas or process improvement proposals; and8) Personality differences;c) Between shifts;1) Work handover not accomplished or done poorly or hurriedly;2) Inadequate record of work accomplished – incomplete stage sheets;3) Processes not documented for all shifts to use; and4) Job boards or check-off lists not kept up to date;d) Between crew and lead;1) Lead fails to communicate important information to crew;
```

### 387

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落387`

- 字数: 2037 (约1222 tokens)

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-20 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigation2) Poor verbal handover or job assignment at the beginning of a shift;3) Unclear roles and responsibilities;4) Lead does not provide feedback to crew on performance;5) Crew fails to report problems and opportunities for improvement to lead; and6) Communication tools (written, phones, radios, etc.) not used;e) Between lead and management;1) Little or no communication exists;2) Goals and plans not discussed regularly;3) No feedback from management to lead on performance;4) Lead does not report problems and opportunities for improvement to management; and5) Management fails to communicate important information to lead; andf) Other;1) Computer or network malfunctions lead to loss of information;2) E-mail not used or ignored; and3) Computer-based manuals and systems not available at outstations.14.4 MAINTENANCE GROUP14.4.1 Air carrier accidents and serious incidents are often investigated by a team. In a team investigation, anairworthiness specialist is usually assigned as maintenance group chairperson to manage the group. The chairperson incoordination with the Investigator-in-Charge (IIC) defines the precise composition of the group.Composition14.4.2 At a minimum, the group should consist of the group chairperson, representatives of the statesparticipating in the investigation, and advisers from the airframe and powerplant manufacturer. Representatives and/oradvisers from other parties to the investigation should be included as needed. Parties assigned to the maintenancegroup should be able to address powerplant, structures, systems, and operations questions that will arise.14.4.3 The group chairperson should consider including an individual from either the operator’s maintenancequality assurance or maintenance engineering department. The individual assigned must have the ability to assist indeciphering airplane maintenance log write-ups and have an excellent understanding of the operator’s maintenanceprogramme and general maintenance manual.
```

### 388

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落388`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 3318 (约1990 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-2114.4.4 It is helpful to include one of the company’s technical personnel and a certificated mechanic/engineer(employed by the operator or third party provider) be placed on standby to assist in explaining any unclear or confusingwrite-ups in the records. Mechanics, more than anyone else, will be able to decipher and explain log book, task card andother write-ups. They understand the culture and “tribal wisdom” of the maintenance community and an awareness ofsub-rosa maintenance practices. They are aware of the idiosyncrasies of the design as well as work-arounds to chronicproblems that never seem to be reported.14.4.5 Finally the group chairperson should request that the owner/operator or contract Maintenance RepairOrganization (MRO) facility place a person responsible for maintenance records on call. There will be numerousrequests for data retrieval and clarification.Duties and responsibilities14.4.6 The maintenance group is responsible for reviewing all maintenance records to expose the service andmaintenance history of the aircraft involved in an accident.14.4.7 The collected data will be studied to determine the effectiveness of the maintenance performed and theadequacy and its relevance to issues associated with the accident. The significance of improper or inadequatemaintenance, servicing, or inspection of an aircraft becomes most evident after a thorough review of the relevant records.14.4.8 These data may indicate a need to explore further any records relative to the aircraft type underinvestigation. They may also suggest lines of investigation to other groups. Correspondingly, data collected by othergroups may suggest lines of investigation for the maintenance group.14.4.9 The maintenance group chairperson will alert the investigative team to any system or component thatbecomes suspect through the records review. In this manner, the group will reduce the potential for overlooking possiblesystem or “hardware”-related accident causes.14.4.10 Investigators must consider operator differences and the regulations that govern them. Large air carriers,commuter air carriers, on-demand air taxi, and operators of large aircraft are regulated by a body of regulations that isrigid and thorough. Corporate- or VIP-use airplanes are frequently operated under General Operating rules. It is,therefore, important to review the approved maintenance programme with respect to the applicable operating regulations.14.4.11 The adequacy of a maintenance programme should never be assumed based upon the size or apparentsophistication of its operation or records system.Specific focus14.4.12 During the investigative process, the maintenance group should focus, as necessary, on the followingspecific objectives:a) Collecting sufficient maintenance history to serve as a reference for all members of the investigativeteam.b) Researching and evaluating the maintenance aspects of specific issues presented to the maintenancegroup by other group chairpersons or the Investigator-in-Charge (IIC).c) Analyzing previous maintenance activities and trends associated with the accident aircraft in anattempt to uncover issues that may not be discernible to other groups because of the destruction ofsystems and structures evidence.
```

### 389

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落389`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 3060 (约1836 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-22 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigationd) Reviewing the operator’s, repair station’s, and contract maintenance provider’s programmes, policies,procedures, and work environment to determine whether any of these may have contributed to theaccident.e) Evaluating responsible regulatory agencies’ oversight of the operator to determine whether it mayhave contributed to the accident sequence.Note.— Operations and maintenance of aircraft may have regulatory oversight by various governmentagencies other than the aeronautical technical regulation organizations, for example, hazardous materials, security,health and safety and environmental agencies.Coordination14.4.13 The group must maintain close coordination with the IIC. The investigation may well point to significantareas of investigation by other specialists. In turn it may be necessary for the maintenance investigation to studyparticular aspects which have come to the attention of others engaged elsewhere in the investigation.14.4.14 The group’s duties require coordination with the operator. An investigation may extend into design,certification, manufacturing, and/or maintenance management. This may include standards and procedures, qualityassurance, equipment and facilities, maintenance personnel selection, training, materials control and regulatoryoversight issues.14.4.15 Before reviewing any records, provide each group member an index sheet delineating the Air TransportAssociation (ATA) chapter codes. Almost every piece of data reviewed will reference these codes. Divide the workinggroup by assigning each person certain relevant ATA codes.14.4.16 Coordinate with the IIC to decide the extent and priorities of the review. Decide on documents to be copiedor data to be extracted for the group report.14.4.17 Hold daily progress meetings to make the group aware of the entire effort.14.4.18 It may be necessary for the group chairperson to discuss the areas that their particular group will befocusing on and the type of expertise needed by members of the group.Operator maintenance programme briefing14.4.19 As soon as possible after convening the group, the group chairperson should advise the owner/operatorthat the group will need to be briefed on the operator’s approved maintenance programme. A complete understanding ofan operator’s maintenance programme is essential. It will normally be necessary to enlist the assistance of the operatorand, in the case of accidents involving aircraft of another State, coordination with that State’s Accredited Representativeis necessary.14.4.20 Before reviewing documentation that relates specifically to maintenance performed on the accident aircraftor the operator’s maintenance programme, it is essential that the operator brief the group about the maintenanceprogramme and activities they are about to review.14.4.21 The group chairperson and the operator should establish the agenda for this briefing. Consider thefollowing topics:a) Organization of the maintenance department including functional relationships.
```

### 390

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落390`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 2905 (约1743 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-23b) Type of maintenance programme authorized and under what body of regulation it is performed.c) Scheduled inspection programme. Include type of checks, time intervals, locations where checks areperformed, description of splitting checks into sub phases or intervals, and a list of those checksperformed by contract maintenance providers.d) Contract maintenance services including the scope and limitations of the services, method ofcoordination for on-call maintenance, operator over-sight/guidance, records movement and tracking ofwork performed.e) Minimum Equipment List (MEL) programme. Include tracking process, notifications, clearingprocedures, etc.f) Airworthiness Directive (AD) compliance programme. Include tracking system, repetitive inspectioncompliance methods, procedure for converting applicable portions of ADs to engineering orders (EO)or engineering authorizations (EA).g) Records system. Include type of approved system, description of supplemental systems, and methodof data collection/entry, tracked items/events, and information retrieval/printout capabilities.h) Stores department policies and procedures, particularly suspected unapproved parts programme,inventory control, segregation of airworthy and non-airworthy parts and oversight of vendors.i) Certification Maintenance Requirements (CMR) and Required Inspection Items (RII) control.14.5 AIR CARRIER ACCIDENTSNotification of owner/operator14.5.1 Immediately after notification of the accident and before proceeding, the group chairperson should ensurethat either the group chairperson or the IIC advise the airplane owner/operator that any and all maintenance records andmanuals pertaining to the accident airplane are impounded. These must be retained and available for review,photographing or copying when requested by the group.Note.— Many operators use a complex system of computerized record keeping in addition to theirregulatory required/approved record programme. These also must be available. This is important to emphasize.14.5.2 Normally records for the previous 90 days are requested. However, the records may in some instancesextend back to the original manufacture of the airplane.14.5.3 Unless specified otherwise in the State regulations, the owner/operator is responsible for the publicationsand records’ safekeeping until the group chairperson or the chairperson’s representative arrives to take possession. Insome situations, the group chairperson may want to request that a local regulatory agency office take possession ofrecords until the group arrives.14.5.4 Manuals obtained must be versions that are currently released by the operator and manufacturers as ofthe date of the accident.14.5.5 It is not necessary for the investigating agency to retain original documents as long as suitable copies ofnecessary records are obtained.
```

### 391

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落391`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 2415 (约1449 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-24 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigation14.5.6 After the preliminary steps have been taken to impound the records and establish the group membership,the chairperson should proceed to the maintenance facility or other location of the records.Identify airplane, powerplant and propeller14.5.7 As soon as possible after the accident have the owner/operator complete the Airplane Data form. TheAppendix to this Chapter shows typical information to record as soon as possible.Maintenance certification14.5.8 Copies of the Operations Specifications shall be available. These define the operator’s scheduledmaintenance programme including limitations and special authorizations as well as the operator’s weight and balancecontrol programme.Maintenance recordsNote.— The physical size of the records can be overwhelming. Therefore, all of the following records neednot be physically presented to the maintenance group but must be available to them upon request. As the line ofinvestigation develops, specific records will come into play.14.5.9 Records to consider for review include the following examples.a) Airplane technical logs and, in some instances, flight attendant logs for the last 90 days. As there areno industry standards for reporting, most logbook entries and reporting disciplines vary from airline toairline and the number of entries cannot be considered indicative of product quality. However, repeatwrite-ups may indicate a problem with the aircraft.b) Aircraft maintenance history data for the last 120 days.c) Routine and non-routine task cards. All non-routine task cards for the last scheduled check and for thelast “C” or “D” level check.Note.— For a transport-category aircraft, there will be hundreds of cards from these checks. Non￾routine task cards will yield a wealth of information and suggest lines of investigation.d) All repair records for the aircraft’s engines, propellers, and primary system components.e) Conditional inspection history for life of aircraft. Examples include such occurrences as overweightlandings, bird strike, lightning strike, aircraft damage reports, etc.f) Any maintenance or servicing before final flight including fuelling slips.g) A list of major repairs and alterations.h) A list of all Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) work that has been accomplished on the accidentaircraft.i) Engine condition monitoring data for the last 30 days.
```

**📊 元数据**

- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-25j) Engine change log. This provides a history of those airplanes on which a given engine has beeninstalled.k) Engine and airframe vibration monitoring data.l) List of Minimum Equipment List (MEL) items currently being carried on the accident aircraft.m) Report of in-flight shutdowns of powerplants covering the last year.n) Configuration Deviation List (CDL) items currently being carried on the aircraft. CDL items areidentified in Appendix CDL of the Approved Flight Manual (AFM).Note.— CDL items define a certificated configuration for the aircraft. As such they do not requirerectification like MEL items require.o) All Airworthiness Directives (ADs) applicable to the accident aircraft. This should include the ADcompliance record of the aircraft indicating repetitive inspections and/or terminating action.p) Service Difficulty Reports (SDR). This is a data collection system intended to be used to provide analert to incipient safety problems with the aircraft. This feedback system encompasses a wide networkof contributors.q) List service bulletins/letters, issued by manufacturers, applicable to the accident aircraft and itscomponents.r) List cancellations/diversions/deviations for the past 6 months.s) Relevant incidents for the accident aircraft (and all others of the same model) for the last 6 months. Ifpossible, have data listed separately for each maintenance station.t) Weight and balance records of the accident aircraft including the trim sheet calculation for the takeoffprior to the accident.Note.— Trim sheet calculations are usually held with flight operations records.Publications14.5.10 Publications required for large aircraft vary with the type of operating rules under which the aircraft wasbeing operated. A complete set of all required publications is huge. The documents are published in several formats –hard copy, microfilm, microfiche, CDs, or web based. Information relating to the detailed maintenance review can befound in the operator’s General Maintenance Manual.Note.— The physical size of these documents can be overwhelming. Therefore, all of the followingpublications need not be physically presented to the maintenance group but must be available to them upon request. Asthe line of investigation develops, specific maintenance publications will come into play.14.5.11 At a minimum the group should start with a copy of the operators’ General Maintenance Manual and theaircraft maintenance manual. The following publications are characteristic of aircraft operated under air carrier rules:
```

- 所属条号: `段落393`

- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-26 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigationa) Maintenance publications consist of the airframe, powerplant, and component manufacturers’publications. Examples include maintenance, structural repair, illustrated parts, software controls,overhaul, NDT manuals, component maintenance manuals, maintenance bulletins, etc. Thepublications reviewed must have been applicable to the aircraft on the date of the accident.b) The appropriate regulatory agency usually requires the operator have a manual system that includesmaintenance policies and procedures. This material is frequently contained in a document called theGeneral Maintenance Manual (GMM). This is an important document for the investigator tounderstand the operator’s maintenance programme. It is a basic reference and should be obtainedearly in the investigation and reviewed by all members of the Maintenance Investigation Group.c) Copies of all service publications such as service bulletins, all operator letters, maintenance tips, andbulletins issued by the operator applicable to the accident airplane.d) All maintenance package task cards applicable at the time of the accident or at the time the lastparticular check was accomplished.The maintenance reviewGeneral14.5.12 The purpose of the maintenance review is to determine:a) that work done has been in accordance with current publications including task cards;b) that work done and inspections done were accomplished in accordance with the approvedmaintenance programme;c) the adequacy of regulatory oversight;d) if a suspect part is authentic or is an unapproved part; ande) that a part is approved/valid for installation on a particular aircraft.These may settle uncertainties or suggest lines of investigation.Initial review14.5.13 Review the operator’s maintenance programme to develop a working understanding of it.14.5.14 Perform a cursory review of the accident aircraft’s maintenance log entries and maintenance historyprintout for the last 30 days. This review should focus on maintenance discrepancies that appear to relate to systemsthat are tentatively suspect based on the limited accident sequence information already available.Drug testing14.5.15 Determine if either the operator or a contract maintenance provider performed maintenance actions on theaircraft in the last few days preceding the accident. If they have, now is the time to determine if drug testing of theindividuals who performed the work will be requested.
```

### 394

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落394`

- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-27Weight and balance14.5.16 Coordinate this activity with the Flight Operations Group.14.5.17 As a part of the initial review, confirm the correctness of weight and balance calculations and stabilizer trimsetting for the accident aircraft prior to its last flight. This record may be in the flight/dispatch records. Check also that theaircraft was correctly loaded.14.5.18 Check the compliance date, location, and method used for the last weighing check. If electronic scaleswere used to weigh the aircraft, check the method and date of calibration and certification.14.5.19 Audit the last recorded weighing report for correctness. This should include confirmation in the record ofweight and centre of gravity changes made to the aircraft since its last weighing.14.5.20 Confirm that changes to the weighing report were conveyed to appropriate flight operations personnel.Detailed review14.5.21 The detailed review should be pursued after the other groups have had time to gather sufficient evidenceto suggest some specific lines of inquiry. In addition to the group’s review of the accident aircraft’s maintenance history,the following programmes, policies, and conditions should be considered for review as dictated by the investigations lineof inquiry.Maintenance programme, policies14.5.22 This material is usually found in the General Maintenance Manual. Contained in this document is found themaintenance department’s organization, duties and responsibilities including individual group functions such as theinspection programme, stores control, weight and balance control, materials control, records administration, engineering,etc.14.5.23 This manual may be composed of separate documents addressing specific topics. Examples: fuelling, de￾icing, minimum equipment list, etc.Management and regulatory oversight14.5.24 Some aircraft accidents have resulted from organizational defects or weaknesses in management and/orregulatory oversight; for example, an operator may have prescribed or condoned procedures not commensurate withsafe operating conditions in practice. Similarly, ambiguous instructions, and those capable of dual interpretation mayalso have existed; these factors may well have stemmed in the first instance from uncritical scrutiny by regulatingauthorities. It may therefore be necessary to inquire closely into other organizations or agencies not immediately ordirectly concerned with the circumstances of the accident but where action, or lack of it, may have permitted or evencaused the accident to happen.14.5.25 Review oversight of the operator or contract repair station by regulatory agency of the state of registry.a) Look at the work programme of the principal maintenance inspector (PMI). Document the extent of thePMI’s responsibilities, percentage of time spent with this operator, percentage of time spent in eachmajor area, and any assistance provided by other inspectors (to include geographic inspectors).
```

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-28 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigationb) Evaluate the qualifications and experience of the PMI and any assigned assistant PMIs. Include in thisa review the subject individuals’ pre- (name of regulatory agency) maintenance experience.c) Review the reports of PMI’s required inspections, inspection status, inspection information/comments,and any trend data for this operator. Review any action letters between the PMI and the operator.d) For an operator, if the state conducts formal oversight audits, review these reports. For example, theUnited States’ regulatory agency, the FAA, conducts a National/Regional Aviation Safety InspectionProgram (NASIP or RASIP).e) Interview line-maintenance, hangar-maintenance, shop maintenance and local regulatory agencyinspectors not associated with the involved operator to gain insight into the PMI’s working relationshipwith the operator’s supervisory maintenance personnel.Inspection programme14.5.25 This should include review of required inspection items (RII), buy-back procedures, vendor control,materials control, continuing analysis and surveillance and return to service.Reliability programme14.5.27 How does the operator identify and track repeat write-ups, line and hangar maintenance rejects (completedmaintenance tasks that were determined to be unacceptably performed at inspection sign-off), and “infant mortality” ofaircraft parts (i.e. recently designed parts with limited service experience determined to be not airworthy when receivednew from the manufacturer). Obtain a copy of the operator’s programme.Tool control programme14.5.28 Determine how personal and company-owned tools are accounted for after each shift change. Determinehow a tool is tracked when temporarily at another base. Review procedures followed when a tool appears to be missing.Find out what type of inspection the operator performs to make sure personal tool boxes do not contain loose, excess, orunapproved hardware.Shift-change procedures14.5.29 Shift change policies vary widely. Numerous accidents have been ascribed to poor or no work handover.Determine how workers on the oncoming shift know where the previous shift stopped work in the performance of anyuncompleted maintenance tasks. Ensure the programme is really being used and that it identifies any components orhardware disconnected or removed simply to gain access to the component being worked on.Scheduled inspection programme14.5.30 This is found in the Maintenance Operations Specifications. Review the programme to ensure that all itemsfor the accident aircraft were being inspected at required intervals.
```

```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-29Functional Check Flight (FCF) programme14.5.31 Determine what types of maintenance actions require an FCF. Review the operator’s programme for FCFpilot qualification, maintenance technician participation and qualification, flow chart/checklist usage, and documentationof data and final airworthiness determinations.Supplemental Structural Inspection Programme (SSIP)14.5.32 Review the operator’s supplemental and corrosion prevention control programme. Make certain therequired reports are being sent to the Aircraft Certification Office (ACO) and manufacturer for findings of level 2 and 3corrosion. Review the aging aircraft inspection status for the fleet.Maintenance training programme14.5.33 Look at the in-house training programme for engine, airframe and systems, to include curriculum, instructorqualification/training, participation percentage, recurrent training, training on special systems, and record-keeping.Determine percentage of participation in manufacturer’s resident training courses. Determine workers’ opinion of initialand recurrent training.Stores programme14.5.34 If a specific off-the-shelf part is suspect, review the programme by which the operator receives, inspects,and incorporates parts into its system. Review the documentation to make sure that the suspect part was “approved”and “airworthy” when installed on the aircraft. Be alert to cure-dated items. Check that parts, especially small parts suchas rivets, bolts and seals, were issued correctly from an approved store and were not obtained from an uncontrolledsource such as a toolbox.Foreign Object Damage (FOD) programme14.5.35 Review programme for hangar, line maintenance and last departure airport for the aircraft. Determine if theprogrammes are actually being used. Check not only the operator’s programme but also the last departure airport’sprogramme.Publications14.5.36 Remember that the actual documents used by individuals involved in the accident may not berepresentative of the most recent version. This is attributable to a number of factors:a) distribution delays of revisions to user locations. Most common are printed, microfiche, microfilm,digital;b) temporary revisions which are easily misplaced or may not be available;c) maintenance tips or bulletins (manufacturer’s as well as the operator’s) that are not rigidly controlled;d) software media that is not rigidly controlled;
```

```text
III-14-30 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident Investigatione) printed revisions which may be incorrectly posted;f) use of materials from previously made copies which are stored in personal tool boxes. make sure thatthis practice is prohibited by the operator; andg) locating manual material in locations inconvenient for users.14.5.37 Finally be aware of personal notes kept by individual technicians that may or may not be reflective ofcurrent published maintenance practices, such as training notes or personal ‘black books’. Also check that up-datessuch as manual revisions, service bulletins and airworthiness directives are available and that time is available for thetechnician to review these periodically.14.5.38 The review should assure that:a) all applicable publications used by maintenance personnel are up to date as of the date of theaccident;b) all applicable publications are easily available to technicians;c) manuals and task cards were used and followed;d) instructions and task cards, including illustrations, are clear and unambiguous. It is helpful to interviewtechnicians for their opinions;e) Illustrations and diagrams are reflective of the aircraft and correct build-up of installed parts. This isvery important as frequently the only build-up information available to technicians is that shown inmanuals (IPC, etc.) which present build-up part orientation in an exploded view;f) Illustrations warn of possible ambiguity when displaying installation and orientation in order to avoidreversed controls;g) aircraft applicability is correct; andh) instructions reflect the incorporation of AD notes, engineering orders and service bulletins applicableto the accident aircraft.Maintenance records14.5.39 The task of reviewing the maintenance records including those carried in the aircraft and those recordsheld at stations through which the aircraft passed is normally conducted at the operator’s station holding the records.14.5.40 In addition to the records that the operator is obliged by regulation to retain, there may be other operationalrecords which could yield valuable information. The investigator should determine if there are any such records retainedby the operator but not required by the regulation.14.5.41 The investigation should examine all maintenance performed on the aircraft just before the accident and, indoing so, determine whether or not it was done properly.14.5.42 Maintenance actions accomplished and inspections performed should be reviewed during this process.Scheduled inspections (such as preflight, service checks, post flight, phase checks, and periodic inspections, conditionalinspections, and modifications) should be reviewed to determine if improper actions caused or contributed to the
```

### 398

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落398`

- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-31accident. Aircraft records should be carefully reviewed to determine proper and accurate documentation of maintenanceactions. Identification of personnel involved in maintenance actions should be done from the aircraft records as well asfrom interviews. Interviews should be conducted with technicians, lead person, supervisors and shop managers. Notethat If several people, such as an engine change crew, are involved in a job, or the job goes over a shift change, thenames in the records only identify some of the people.Aircraft technical and flight attendant logs14.5.43 Review the previous log entries for the incident aircraft for the last 90 days.14.5.44 Make sure to record the station identifier, mechanic’s identification number, and ATA chapter code for anysuspicious write-ups or corrective actions.14.5.45 Repeated technical log entries against a specific system or LRU may be indicative of either a problem withthe aircraft or the LRU.14.5.46 There have occurred instances involving airworthiness items that have been entered into the flightattendant logs. The operator may not have a system for screening the flight attendant log. The consequence is that thediscrepancy may be missed by the maintenance community and go uncorrected.Task cards14.5.47 If there is a suspect system or component, the routine task cards signed off during the last applicableinspection should be requested. Each action box on the relevant card should be reviewed for inspection findings andcorrective actions taken.14.5.48 Note that for a transport category aircraft, there will probably be hundreds of non-routine task cards fromthe last scheduled check and from the last “C” or “D” level check.Conditional inspection history14.5.49 Because these inspections are only performed if the aircraft has experienced a special or unusualcondition, it is important to search the records for the life of the aircraft for evidence of damage and repairs.Aircraft damage reports14.5.50 This might be the only place that will indicate if the aircraft was damaged e.g., service truck colliding withengine pylon while aircraft is parked at gate. Pay particular attention to the possible ground damage imposed upon theaircraft by servicing personnel.Contract maintenance before final flight14.5.51 Talk directly to the shop supervisor to determine if work was performed. The operator may not yet beaware of all contract actions prior to the aircraft’s last flight.
```

### 399

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落399`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 3003 (约1801 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-32 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident InvestigationServicing14.5.52 Review servicing procedures, particularly fuelling and de-icing, accomplished prior to the aircraft’s lastflight.Major repairs and alterations14.5.53 Review the list of all Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) work that has been accomplished on theaccident aircraft. In one case in which the aircraft experienced an in-flight loss of control, reviewing this list helped todetermine that the accident aircraft was the only one in the operator’s entire fleet with the newest thrust reversermodification.Powerplant data14.5.54 a) Engine condition monitoring data for the last 30 days. There may be a formal or informal programmeor just untracked data recorded on the aircraft maintenance log. If you are provided raw data only, askthe operator if records can display the data in a graphic format.b) Engine change log. This log will show you which aircraft within the operator’s fleet the engines on theaccident aircraft have been on in the past. If there is a questionable engine, you can review itsmaintenance history (by engine ATA code) for the period it was on another aircraft.c) Engine and airframe vibration monitoring data.14.5.55 Minimum equipment list. List of MEL items that were being carried on the accident aircraft. Determine fromthe master MEL the category (A, B, C, or D) of any carried items and whether any deferred items are past the allowabletime limit.Airworthiness directive (AD) note list14.5.56 Review all ADs applicable to the accident aircraft. Confirm compliance date and methods. If there is asuspected problem with a component or system that has any ADs written against it, review a copy of the EngineeringOrder that was written by the operator to carry out the applicable portions of the AD. For those ADs which do not have orthat have not had terminating action accomplished examine records of required repetitive actions14.5.57 Airworthiness Directive research is not easy. The investigator must be certain to search the aircraft, engine,propeller, and appliance indexes, published supplements and the ADs themselves. However, some ADs can hide fromeven the best index or search engine. For example, there is an appliance AD on paper air filters that applies tothousands of small aircraft. You can easily find this AD by performing a search for “paper filter”, but you would probablynot think to do so. There is no substitute for experience and intuition when researching ADs.14.5.58 Many large aircraft have been operated in different states of registry. When researching ADs, search notonly those ADs applicable to the current registry of the aircraft but also ADs which may have been applicable underprevious registry.Certification Maintenance Requirements (CMR)14.5.59 Review the record of accomplishment. Assure that the CMR requirements were completed within therequired time. Review the task cards associated with a CMR and any non-routine work that may have arisen from suchCMR checks.
```

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-33Service Difficulty Reports (SDR)14.5.60 Review for any suspect component reports. Be very specific and narrow the request as much as possible.There may be thousands of reports for a specific model of aircraft. Consider the limitations of SDRs previouslydiscussed.14.5.61 Consider that many judge these data of little value because:a) they are virtually never the first source for identifying safety problems in transport aircraft;b) the data are of low quality and are inconsistent. They frequently lack key information;c) reporting requirements are too vague, leading to varying interpretations of what should be reported;d) aircraft manufacturers provide oral and written service advice on malfunction incidents, serviceproblems, and potential solutions that are timelier than SDR data. Moreover, the quantity and varietyof data provided to the aviation industry, by the manufacturers, not only match but far exceed those ofthe SDR programme. Additional types of data provided by the manufacturers include technicalinformation on inspections, modifications and repairs, advice to customers of current events, and tipsto simplify troubleshooting and maintenance; ande) caution must be applied to the interpretation of the data. It contains unverified information whosequality is highly dependent on the reporter’s knowledge, experience, and judgment. Be very specificand narrow this request as much as possible. There may be thousands of reports for a specific aircraftmodel.Service bulletins/letters,14.5.62 Search for titles that apply to the accident aircraft including suspect components.Record of cancellations, diversions, deviations14.5.63 Search for the accident aircraft (and all others of the same model) in the fleet for the last six months. Ifpossible, have data listed separately for each maintenance base. This may indicate a trend.Part authentication14.5.64 Following the identification of a part as possibly having relevance to the probable cause of the accident it isnecessary to authenticate its:a) airworthiness;b) correct installation; andc) maintenance history.14.5.65 There are numerous sources of parts that do not meet applicable requirements but enter the aviationsystem. All too frequently accidents have been attributed to improper parts or improperly installed parts that are eitherthe root cause or contributory probable cause of the accident.
```

### 401

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落401`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 3418 (约2050 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-34 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident InvestigationAirworthiness14.5.66 The concept of airworthiness of an aeronautical product is that the product conforms to an approveddesign and is in a condition for safe operation. In order for the product to continue to be airworthy, any replacement ormodification parts installed must also conform to an approved design.14.5.67 The airworthiness of an aeronautical product containing unapproved parts is questionable because thepart’s type design and quality are unknown. Positive identification of unapproved parts is often difficult, due to thesimilarity of unapproved part’s characteristics to those of approved parts.14.5.68 As a part of determining the airworthiness of a part it is necessary to establish its origins. The investigatorshould be familiar with Suspect Unapproved Parts programmes.14.5.69 Particular attention must be directed to determine that the part is not counterfeit.14.5.70 As you get into smaller general aviation aircraft the chain of evidence becomes less detailed. Sometimesthe genesis of the part is obscure particularly with aircraft that have had many operators or owners and differentregistries. Particularly, changing the state of registry blurs the applicable airworthiness and maintenance rules whichmay apply. Suspect unapproved parts becomes an important issue.Unapproved parts14.5.71 Parts fall into two broad informal categories: approved and unapproved. Collectively, these parts arereferred to as “unapproved parts” and, similarly, “approved parts” is the term for parts that do meet all applicablerequirements.14.5.72 The commonly used term “approved part” is not synonymous with “a part that has received a formalregulatory approval.” The terms “approved parts” and “unapproved parts” as used here are not legal definitions, butsimply a reflection of the need to have a broad term that identifies parts that should, or should not, be installed on anaircraft. Parts that can be used on an aircraft (i.e. “approved parts”) are described as parts “acceptable for installation” or“eligible for installation”.Counterfeit parts14.5.73 Counterfeit parts are a pernicious type of “unapproved part.” They are parts made or altered so as toimitate or resemble an “approved part” without authority or right, and with the intent to mislead or defraud by passing theimitation as original or genuine. They may be new parts that are deliberately misrepresented as designed and producedunder an approved system or other acceptable method even though they were not so designed and produced.14.5.74 Counterfeit parts may also be used parts that, even though they were produced under an approved system,have reached a design life limit or have been damaged beyond possible repair for aviation standards, but are alteredand deliberately misrepresented as acceptable, with the intent to mislead or defraud.Salvaged repairable parts14.5.75 It is quite common, when an aircraft is out of production and spare parts are not available, to acquire a partfrom a salvage yard. This is particularly true with structural replacement parts. Be suspicious of life-limited parts whichhave been salvaged.14.5.76 If a used part is thought to be worth saving for future use under controlled conditions it is salvageable.These parts should be documented and controlled so that they are not returned to service until they meet allairworthiness requirements.
```

### 402

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落402`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 3456 (约2073 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-3514.5.77 If a used part is not thought to be worth saving, it is “scrap,” and should be disposed of in such a way that itcannot be returned to aviation service. However, both salvageable and scrap parts are sometimes misrepresented ashaving useful time left or as having been repaired in accordance with regulations.14.5.78 Other examples of parts that are not eligible for use are parts rejected during the production processbecause of defects, parts for which required documentation has been lost, parts that have been improperly maintained,and parts from military aircraft that have not been shown to comply with civil regulatory requirements. None of theseshould be installed in an aircraft.14.5.79 “Unapproved parts” also occur when a supplier that produces parts for an approved manufacturer shipsdirectly to end users without the approved manufacturer’s authorization or a separate, applicable Parts ManufacturerApproval (PMA). An example of this is “production overrun” parts. Because these parts are not authorized by theProduction Approval Holder (PAH), one cannot assume that they have met all the requirements of the approval holder’srequired quality control process; therefore, they are in contravention of the regulations and are not authorized for use onaeronautical products.14.5.80 It is evident that the concept of “unapproved part” is wide ranging, encompassing everything from partsthat are improperly maintained, to parts produced under regulatory approval but shipped without proper documentation,to parts deliberately and criminally misrepresented as “approved parts.”Proof of authenticity or of past ownership14.5.81 In the case of Line Replaceable Units (LRUs) the genesis of the part may be traced by using the part’sairworthiness approval tags, serviceable tags and removal tags. Removal tags are those normally attached to removedLRUs that indicate its status as being airworthy, repairable, or condemned.14.5.82 If the accident aircraft, its components, engines or propellers were imported in the recent past, review allimport process documentation and the actions taken qualifying the items to be in service. You may be required tocontact and possibly interview the involved regulatory personnel who handled this process. It may be necessary tocontact the regulatory office that provided oversight of foreign repair facilities used by the operator.14.5.83 Part identification and control procedures are covered in the operator’s general maintenance manual (GMM).Parts broker or dealers14.5.84 Parts brokers or dealers are not regulated. Often salvaged parts from other accident aircraft find their wayinto this supply chain.Borrowed14.5.85 Part borrowing is a common practice. Its use frequently requires specific approval from the regulatoryagency. Determine that the operator and part lender are authorized to borrow parts. Consult both operators’ operationsspecifications. The general maintenance manual of the operators will contain the procedures for borrowing parts.Parts Manufacturing Authorization (PMA)14.5.86 If the part was produced under a PMA, determine the details of the PMA and its authenticity.Supplemental Type Certificate (STC)14.5.87 Determine details of an STC including its authenticity. Include a determination that the incident aircraft is apart of the STC and that the STC is valid in the state of registry of the incident aircraft.
```

### 403

**📊 元数据**
- 所属条号: `段落403`
- 章节路径: `-`
- 字数: 2936 (约1761 tokens)
- 分块类型: paragraph

**📝 核心内容**
```text
III-14-36 Manual of Aircraft Accident and Incident InvestigationOriginal Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)14.5.88 Was the part installed at the time of aircraft manufacture? This may be determined from the aircraftdelivery records and provisioning records.14.5.89 With the passage of time, information may be obtained from the operator’s stores department records suchas purchase order and/or receiving inspection records.14.5.90 When the aircraft involved is out of production, OEMs are a frequent source for structural “spares.” Oftenthese structural parts are removed from retired aircraft and sold as is. The purchaser is required to establishairworthiness of these salvaged parts.In-house manufactured parts14.5.91 Parts not formally approved by regulatory agencies that are acceptable if used in the proper application areparts “fabricated” by maintenance personnel in the course of their repair work for the purpose of returning a product(aircraft, engine, or propeller) to service. Such parts are still required to meet applicable design criteria. Examplesinclude sheet metal parts, tubing and cable assemblies, etc.Items resulting from AD note compliance14.5.92 A service bulletin attached to the AD will frequently call up a kit of parts or it may call for local manufactureof an item.Standard hardware14.5.93 Is the attaching hardware correct? It is not uncommon to use, consciously or unconsciously, hardware likenuts, bolts and other fasteners that do not conform to aeronautical standards.14.5.94 Manufacturers of products such as airframes and engines often specify that it is acceptable to use“standard parts,” such as nuts and bolts, for production and maintenance of those products. Standard parts production isnot specifically monitored by the regulatory agencies, but must conform to specified industry-accepted criteria. Standardparts can be tested for conformity and may be used in aeronautical products only when specified in the type design.14.5.95 These include an established industry or acceptable international specification which contains performancecriteria, test and acceptance criteria, and uniform identification requirements. The specification must include allinformation necessary to produce the part, and be published so that any party may manufacture the part. Examplesinclude, but are not limited to, U.S. National Aerospace Standards (NAS), Army-Navy Aeronautical Standard (AN), andSociety of Automotive Engineers (SAE).Lessors14.5.96 Increasingly many LRUs and type certificated items are leased to operators. The lease may include fullmaintenance support as provided by the lessor.Previous operator14.5.97 Previous owners or operators may have transferred their spares inventory to the new owner/operator. Atthe transfer to a new operating certificate the State of Registry may have been changed. Question whether the partstransferred meet the airworthiness requirements of the new State of Registry.
```

**📝 核心内容**
```text
Part III. InvestigationChapter 14. Maintenance Investigation III-14-37Correct installation14.5.98 Another contributory element to the accident is, all too frequently, the part’s incorrect installation. Was thesuspect part correct for installation in the incident aircraft? Was the orientation of the part correct?Part history14.5.99 There may be several configurations of the part that are effective on specific aircraft or dependencies uponprior accomplishment of specific manufacturer’s service bulletins. Is the installed software correct for the part as well asthe aircraft? If the part in question was not the original installation what was the reason the part was installed on theincident aircraft?a) What is the Mean Time Between Unscheduled Removals (MTBUR) for the part?b) What is the fleet experience with the suspect part?c) What is the characteristic mode of failure?d) What are the maintenance processes for the part?e) Hard time, on condition, condition monitored?f) Is the part life limited?g) Have the time/cycle limits in the operator’s maintenance programme escalated? Is the operatorauthorized escalation?14.6 GENERAL AVIATION AIRCRAFT INVESTIGATIONGeneral14.6.1 The spectrum of general aviation operations and equipment is wide. Vehicle types include ultralights, typecertificated products including gas turbine or piston-powered fixed-wing airplanes, gliders and lighter-than-air machines.They include experimental vehicles such as home-built and restored military vehicles. They may be small experimentalsingle engine aircraft to large transport category aircraft.14.6.2 Operations are conducted by hobbyists, individuals, non-air carrier corporate entities, VIP fleets, andfractional ownership organizations.14.6.3 General aviation is less rigorously regulated than air carriers.Investigation of general aviation accidents14.6.4 Frequently general aviation accidents are not team investigated. The entire investigation may involve oneindividual, with access to specialists as required.
```
